And greetings and welcome to another mastermind session. I'm Eileen Cartwright. I'll be the moderator for this session. Today we have Boss Wilders. He's the CEO of the Chaldeany Institute and a bestselling author. In this session, he will lead us through exploring the principle of contrast and its practical applications in persuasion. Before we begin, please have your microphones muted unless you are called on and feel free to use the chat area to send any questions you have or you can raise your hand if you have a question. Just look at the bottom of your screen and click on reactions to do that. With that said, let's take this deep dive together and I'll turn things over to Boss. Thank you very much, Eileen. Can you please enable my option to share my screen? That will be wonderful. Yes, thank you. Hello, everybody. I see some familiar faces. Good to see you all. Dr. Tecchana, Randy, Mark, Paul, Jeff. Fantastic seeing you all. So indeed today we will dive into the principle of contrast, the contrast phenomenon. And let's start right away because there's a lot of interesting insights to share today. And as you know, we have the seven principles of persuasion, reciprocity, liking, unity, social proof, authority, consistency and scarcity. And as we teach in our Chaldeany certified practitioner program, how can you amplify those principles? Sorry, how can you activate those principles? How can you activate those principles? How can you amplify its effect? Today we're going to talk about the contrast phenomenon and that's very powerful on its own, but it's actually also an amplifier of all the principles. So during this presentation, you see how multiple things can amplify the effects just by saying one sentence. So I want to start with a question. How to sell a Ferrari? It's a high ticket price, it's a luxury car. How do you sell it? And that's obviously a question that the car manufacturer Ferrari asked himself. And what makes sense if you want to sell a car? And that's the same way how Ferrari taught. You sell a car at a car fair, you promote it there and you hope to get interest. Something interesting happens because at a car fair, there are also other brands promoting himself. For example, an average family car like this Suzuki. What happens with the perception of the price of the Ferrari at this moment? The Ferrari is perceived as expensive compared to in this case the Suzuki. So they were wondering, where should we sell our Ferraris? What is a great place to do that? And while I'm telling you, maybe you already have some ideas of your own, the answer is sell the Ferrari at your fairs. Our Ferrari is now perceived. It's cheap compared to a 50, $200 million yard. It's basically a toy for the people who can afford a yard. So besides they use the contrast phenomenon, very smart in this case, they also had the right audience to buy these cars right away. Because if you can afford a $50 million yard or a 200 million yard, you can also pay a 300 or $400,000 Ferrari. So this is a great example on how to use contrast and as a sleut of influence, how can you see where to position your products amongst other products? So today, as Eilid told you in the introduction, let's talk about contrast. And I love this because as I said in the beginning, contrast is an amplifier of each principle. There's not only an amplifier of each principle, it has a very powerful effect on its own. And for a lot of people, contrast in the beginning is a little bit hard to come up with ideas. We are not used most of the time to this phenomenon. So we can dive into very practical ways of thinking. How can you use it for your own influence challenges, for your colleagues, for your clients? So what you will learn today is after this presentation, you can change how anybody experience anything. In the Ferrari example, it was the perception on price. It was much perceived, much less expensive in the yacht fare than in the car fare. As a joke, you can even, if you let's say your single want to go on a date, you can even perceive how people perceive your attractiveness compared to others. There are all kinds of research. So you can change the perception how people experience anything. In other words, you can change the way people experience anything by shaping what they experience first. What they experience first is the anchor. This also is known as the anchor technique. Only I say with the anchor technique, you only do half the work. You create an anchor. But the contrast phenomenon says, you also have to put your product, service, offer, connected to that anchor, to complete the effect and to move more people in your direction to have the maximum persuasiveness effect. So an interesting one is of course money. We have to deal with money and economic circumstances each day. And I want to sketch an example. Imagine you want to buy a new coffee machine and you are in a shop and in that shop, the price is $70 for the coffee machine. But you know, if you walk only two minutes to another shop, the price of this coffee machine is $30. Would you walk two minutes to go down from $70 to $30? Probably you would walk those two minutes. Now we're going to buy a different product. We're going to buy this car. And in one shop, it's $150, $1,150. And if you walk two minutes, it's $151,110. Would you walk? Probably not. And this is fascinating because it's the same $40. You can buy exactly the same things with the same price difference. But in one case, compared to the anchor of $70, $40, is perceived as a lot of money. And compared to the $151,150, it's compared as less valuable. Rationally, it's the same value, but we act completely different. So here is a first ideation. How do you compare when you're making your offers or you talk about money and the experience of money from your target of influence is important? Make sure you have an anchor. And that doesn't have to be a product price every time. For example, you could say, I know if we solve this influence case, it will probably generate millions of dollars extra revenue for you. Now these millions of dollars or $5 million that's even better. That becomes the anchor. Our price for our consulting is just $50,000. So you can play with it. Contrast is also a way of using language. You can embed that in your day-to-day communication as you will see also down the line in this training session. And I have a video here and it's from Dan Ariyeli. I know Dan is on the fire on some, he is accused of forging some data. This is a very famous video. I want to reflect not only what he found here on contrast, but on another topic. And therefore I kept it in. So let's first watch the video only. I think I need to share with sound. So give me a second. And yes, here we go. Share with sound. So first watch it. It's a very famous example. So if you previously saw it, please watch it again because you will see two elements here that are important. Here are two examples of this principle. This was an ad from the economist a few years ago that gave us three choices. An online subscription for $59, a print subscription for 125, or you could get both for 125. Now I looked at this and I called up the economist and I tried to figure out what were they thinking. And they passed me from one person to another to another. Until eventually I got to the person who was in charge of the website and I called them up and they went to check what was going on and the next thing I know the ad is gone. And no explanation. So I decided to do the experiment that I would have loved the economist to do with me. I took this and I gave it to a 100 MIT students. I said, what would you choose? And he's of the market share. Most people wanted the combo deal. Thankfully nobody wanted the dominated options. It means our students can read. But now if you have an option that nobody wants, you would take it off, right? So I printed another version of this when I eliminated the middle option and I gave it to another 100 students. Here's what happens. Now the most popular option became the least popular and the least popular became the most popular. What was happening is that option that was useless in the middle was useless in a sense that nobody wanted it. But it wasn't useless in a sense that it helped people figure out what they wanted. In fact, relative to the option in the middle, which was get only the print 425, the print N-web 425 looked like a fantastic deal. So a very interesting situation of course because this was a real ad from the economists. They offered the print only for 125. We see two things happening here. First of all, the power of contrast. Because indeed, like Dan Ariely claims, if you have an online print only and online plus print is just as expensive as print only, print plus offline, indeed seems like a fantastic deal. So that's how you can use contrast, positioning it between other products while designing behavior in a certain direction. For me, there's a big but here in this example and that's ethics. Of course, as you know, one of our core values is the ethical application of the science of inference. And as you also know from our practitioner program, we have three minimal criteria. Is it true? Well, obviously it was true. They offered this. Is this natural to present your products in that way? Well, then I raise my doubts. It's not natural to price one product and then a product that offers more for the same price. Then is it wise to do that with your people after they've been aware of how they've been treated, want to do business with you? I would argue not. This is called a decoy effect in behavioral science and psychology. A decoy effect is creating an option where you know nobody wants it, but it will make people make different choices just like print only subscription from the economist. So what I want to point out here is, don't learn and copy the economist offer, but learn from the effect what was presented. But do it in an ethical way. We have to come up with offers where you can at least say, yes, we offer it, it's true, but also is it natural? So what could be natural is, you have offer A, offer B, and offer A and B for a discounted rate. That's very natural, wise you can do. But what the economist did, they knew obviously about behavioral science and he created only the option to let more people buy the more expensive option. And then I would draw the ethical line there. So my lesson is two-sided. You see the power of contrast in this example. So put your products next to different products can have a huge impact on the decision that your clients or colleagues will make, but create and position these offers in an ethical way so they're actually making sense that not when people start thinking about it, what's going on, that it will backfire to you. It was still powerful, but definitely it will backfire eventually because it's not natural to present these offers. Then a brilliant quote from our colleague, Dr. Greg Knightet. He always says, if you can't find contrast, you're not looking hard enough. And this is brilliant. So many times we get the question, but what is the most powerful principle or Dr. Cialdini gets this question all the time? Our answer is the principle that is naturally available in the situation. That's the most powerful principle at that moment. Contrast is always available because if you can't find contrast, you're not looking hard enough. You can contrast each and anything. To give you an example, how I faced a challenge in my previous company using contrast, it's about dishwashers, then you might think. Dishwashers, I owned previously a company and we sold kitchens. We had 20 shops in the Netherlands and we were in the business of the budget range. So people, to give you an estimate, had a budget of 4,000, 5,000 euro for the entire kitchen. And they wanted all the equipment. And they did their homework like any other customer would do. And for dishwashers, she often see the energy label, which is nowadays very important, but you also see the noise level. In this example, 44 decibel. And then they found other dishwashers and they were 47 or 48 decibels. We cannot really hear the difference, but it was made very important online by these manufacturers. Oh, this is super silent, it's only 44 decibel. But the price difference was a couple of hundreds of euros per dishwashers. Well, if you're under budget range, that's a lot of money. But this became all the time an argument with our advisors and you don't want getting into arguments with your clients. So I need to crack the code. How can I make that less important besides saying you cannot hear the difference because that didn't do the job. So I came up with a contrast solution. And the solution was in the Netherlands, there's a lot of woods, a lot of forests, and almost everybody sometimes going to walk into forests or in the woods. So I instructed every advisor from us, I say, if this pops up, you ask your clients, do you ever walk in the woods? And you will get a yes. So they say yes. What do you experience there? The people answer, oh, it's nice, peaceful, quiet. Then I have good news for you because the average decibel in a forest is about 50. All these dishwashers are more silent than the forest. And then really that did the job. And we had the research to support that as well. So that did the job. There was no more argument about the importance of the dishwashers and our advisors were still friends with the client and didn't end up in discussions. So this is a great example. I think I compared dishwashers with the decibels of a wood. If you can't find contrast, you're not looking hard enough. I have to admit, this was not a one minute thinking show. For me, I had to push my brain to really come up with different solutions. But that's what we teach, right? We say we have in our practitioner program, we say there are three practitioners, the bungler who don't know the principles, but might have a good offer, but they cannot present it. So nobody gets it. Which basically is a loose, loose situation. We have the smuggler that knows the principles, but uses them unethically to have short term gains. But they lose also in the long term, but in the beginning it's win for them, lose for their clients often. And then we have the sleut of influence. And sleut is a different word for a detective. Why I'm telling this, because you find in contrast, we have to work like this detective. We have to use our brain power and come up with this, because you saw the powerful effect. It was in 20 shops, on average, there were three people per shop. So there were 60 people having this challenge on a daily base. And with one short story, it was gone. So that's a very powerful effect. So that's the first step. Then I want to dive into contrast for pricing, because we present all kinds of offers all day long, especially nowadays, of course, online. What I'm going to show you is one of the most common mistakes and easily correctable mistakes, if you are advising other persons in the online world. This is what you see most of the time, if somebody prices their products. You have a basic, the locks, and a premium version. Look at the order of the pricing. We start with the cheapest. How does the rest, in this case, of packages, would feel to you? They start feeling more and more expensive. And if we have that feeling, we don't move forward so easily, if that thing would feel less and less expensive to us. So what would be the simple solution? I can hear you all think, yes, change the order. And that's exactly what happened in this test. Just change the order of presentation. Remember, this is online, no continuous interaction. It's just the perception of an image. But by doing this, in this AB test, was 31% more revenue. This is absolutely costless to do. And if I analyze, and I analyze a lot of websites, I think still about 70% of all the websites, I see a common mistake, they make their pricing go from low to high. An easy recommendation, an easy AB test, which succeeds in the far majority of the time. Again, it's not black and white science here, but I know from setting this up many times, succeeds in the far majority of the time, just change the order. And this is with pricing, but it could also be done with products. So this is a campaign in the Netherlands from T-Mobile. And they had internet at home, and you had 50 megabytes at that time, 100 and 500 on the left side. Again, packages increased, it becomes more expensive on the left side. So they did the right side, they opened with their biggest and best offer, 500 megabit. Results, 34.4% more sales of the 500 megabit offer, that was been tested here. Why? That becomes the anchor, the starting point. The 500 megabit comes after 50, 100, and then it comes. Now it's perceived as more expensive, the most expensive option. One more example, this is Vodafone Tice, that's a Dutch word for home, and this is an ad, they targeted expats. So the rest of the ad is in English. So this was internet TV and calling from your home for 30 euros per month. The banner ad, click or more information. You would say and argue, this is a normally designed ad, but then they changed it because what you need to do as a sleuth of influence using contrast, you can also answer the question, compared to what? Compared to what 30 euros in this case is a great offer. It's now a standalone offer, there's no comparison. So the ad became this, Vodafone at home now, and they showed what their competitors had to offer. 368% more clicks. So you saw now multiple ways how you can contrast, it connecting it to an offer where price was included, but price was not only the thing which was contrasted. That was in the first example. The second example, the product itself was contrasted, and three in this last example, the competitors were contrasted. So you already see multiple options how to do this. And these are online examples, and it just works just as well, of course, in the offline world. I see Tony, you raise your hand, feel free to unmute yourself. Yeah, so... Great to see you. Only it's often referred to as anchoring, and that's a mistake. That's why I mentioned the term anchoring in the beginning. You could compare it as influence versus persuasion. I just kind of wanted to share that, I agree contrast is something that sometimes people have a difficult time identifying. On the other hand, when people are using it, I think sometimes, and I found this with folks I'm working with, is they understand contrast, but they always think the right order is higher to lower and they get it to work against them. So understanding what you're influencing, like in this particular case here, is prices against your competitor. So if you have the lowest price, you want to make sure you're first and not at the bottom of the list. So I just wanted to share that because I kind of see that a lot. Oh, thanks for that great add on Ezio. That's really valuable, I think, for everybody. And it's funny we had a conversation at the office today, actually, about it, because it was putting some small big videos in order and they had titles about the impact and they heard me presenting this in the morning. So they said, oh, highest first. And I corrected them and say, no, lowest first because you want to increase how powerful this was. That perception, so great add on. So indeed, it's not default always the highest first when you're presenting things. It's what effect you want to maximize and need the effect must be decreasing with your offer. You want to feel your own offers, things feel cheaper. Then yes, high to low. But if you compare towards us, it might be different or an impact you want an increasing line indeed. So great add on, thanks for sharing Ezio. Okay, great. Let's continue to the next part. And that's pricing and I love this study. It's done for low cost items. There's also research for luxury items. Now I show low cost items on pricing and round numbers at the end or an exact number like .99. And there were pens. So people got to choose from two pens. Which one would you choose in condition one? You buy something for $2 or $2.99. $2 or $3. $1.99, $2.99 and $1.99, $3. You see all the time the max difference is two cents. So it's really penis. But the outcome of what people would decide to buy is completely different in this comparison. Look at that. In condition one, the majority, that's 44% chooses the more expensive item. In this case, percentual, it's a 50% more expensive item. But in the price condition four, only 18%. So how do you price condition and present your offers? Again, could have a huge impact on the decision of your customers. This is an interesting one. How do you use round numbers versus rounded up numbers? So in .99. Mark, I see you have a question. Yeah, I don't get your numbers, sorry. So you walk through it again. Yeah, do you see my screen? Yeah, yeah. Okay, so you see here $2 versus $2.99. And the question in condition one is, which product, which pen do you buy? Condition two, $2 versus $3. So it's then a round up both the prices. But you see, this one is all $3, but it's rather presented at $2.99 or only $3. These are all $2, but it's rather presented at $2 or $1.99. And then they compare the multiple, then they compare four different ways of contrasting them versus each other. And they looked how people decided to buy which option. And as you see, there's huge, huge difference. Well, the price isn't a huge difference, it's one cent. But what people choose is a huge difference. So in condition one, 56% of the participants choose pen A and 44, pen B. When it was conditioned for, where the perception is, the biggest price difference, $1.99 versus $3, 82% chooses pen A versus pen B, 80%. Does that help Mark? It does. I was looking at those percentages trying to calculate the price differences, which is not what those numbers were. So yeah, gotcha. Okay, yeah, oh great, yeah, I get it. So yeah, that was a confusion. No, it's how much, what the participants of the experiment choose as the product. And the price difference is basically only one cent every time. So what I also want to start, contrast for negotiation. And that's not only for professional negotiators, actually we are negotiating all the time about things that we buy, that we sell. So what we do for other people, so there's a lot of negotiation going on in communication. We have two interesting tips. In this particular research, it was the sale of a factory. This research was done amongst people that were professionals in this area. And they had the job to sell the factory. There were two conditions. Party that wanted to buy, the factory makes the first offer, or the party wanting to sell makes the first offer. The outcome is very interesting. If the party who wants to buy makes the first offer, it was an average price of 14.5 million euros. Now if the opening bar fit came from the person who wants the party that wants to sell, the average price they agreed to was 18.3 million euros. What's the implication of this study? Control the situation. If you are in a negotiation, you want to be the party that opens first. Why? The first offer is seen as the anchor. So now we have anchoring back. So that's the anchor that's on the table, and people work from there in their mind. So if you are in a negotiation, or whether you're a buyer or the seller, research shows you want to control the opening bit, then you control the anchor. Then another way is what kind of opening bit you want to like. What this research done is people were the seller, sales the sellers of a used car. And they got an opening bit from the buyer. And their job was close the deal because it's pretty close to your desired offer, but try to make it as close as possible. So the buyer got an desired offer, but try to negotiate a higher offer from what you got from your buyer. So the participants were acting as sellers, sorry, yes, they were the sellers and other participants were instructed to be the buyers. The sellers got an offer from the buyer and the seller's job was to negotiate a higher price. The buyer was instructed three different ways. One, open with a round figure, 2000, open with the exact amount of 1865 or 2135, which was both for the same car. So there was a lower opening bit that you could say, well, the car is worth a bit more, and there was a bit that was a bit, a little bit maybe more value for the car. What were the outcomes? The opening bit was a round number. It was an average counter offer that was 23% different from that offer. Or 23% margin. The opening was an exact amount. The average was 10 to 15% different on that opening offer. What's the lesson here? We want the opening's offer. We saw that with selling the factory research. What kind of opening offer you want to do is an exact number. And now we also see another principle coming in. Actually, the principle of authority. By showing exact numbers, we're showing we did our homework, we're showing our credibility there. So this be perceived as a more credible openings offer. And therefore, people stayed closer to that opening's bit than just a round number. What do we do in reality? We do the hard work often, and we come to, let's say, 1996, we say, let's make it 2000. Because that's easy. That's a mistake. Stop doing that in negotiations, in budget presentations, in making offers. Show exact numbers. If you do these things, control the openings bit because your exact number will be perceived as the anchor in your offers. And then I promised examples. How is contrast an amplifier of all principles? And remember, contrast is always available. So you can activate, like we teach in the Chaldeany Certified Practitioner Program, how to activate the principles, how to amplify them. So if you have an idea, you can always amplify the effect using contrast. So to give you a first example, reciprocity, an activated is a gift, a preferable meaning, unexpected and customized. So let's imagine somebody you want to surprise with this gift. You have a football match, and they are very eager to go there, and you have two tickets. So you say, I have two tickets to the match you wanted to see for you. That person would be happy. You would definitely activate reciprocity. How can we use contrast to amplify this? I didn't buy one ticket. I bought two tickets for the match you really wanted to see. Now two is perceived as more because it's not one. And then you could add even in your sentence because then you can go with your spouse, your son, somebody else, so you can build from there. This is a way to use contrast to amplify the gift. Like I said in the beginning, it can become a default way of communicating as you will see me going through these examples. Another one, liking. As we know, compliments are an activator of liking. So I want to give you a compliment for this great achievement. People will like you as we know. How can we use contrast in this case? Normally I don't do this. I really want to give you a compliment for this great achievement. Now I contrasted the compliment. Normally I'm not a person that does this. So the power of this compliment is subtly amplified. Going to unity. I did a little bit of Dutch example as a joke. And I hope as unity for all Dutch people, you will know him. We're all Dutch. So let's support Max Verstappen. For those who don't know Max Verstappen, he's the world champion for Mula Wonka driver. But how can I amplify this if I want to persuade somebody to support him? I could say, you're not German. You're not French. We're all Dutch. That support Max Verstappen. I point out the contrast of not being French, not being German, being Dutch. This is how contrast could amplify a unity. An activator of unity. Sorry. So as you see me going to these examples, I think you will get it. It's using language in a certain way and you can embed that and you can practice this all day long. And you will see the effect. So contrast and social proof. A review score 4.7 out of 5 out of 19,527 reviewers. Great social proof. How can you amplify this with contrast? A review score is 4.7 out of 5, not just based on a couple of hundred reviews, but on 90,527 reviewers. Now these 90,000, the amount is amplified. Look at that. Not just a couple of hundred. These are almost 20,000 reviewers. Next example, authority. I have three master degrees. I don't, but that's a claim. I have three master degrees. It's an example. I don't have just one master degree, but three master degrees. See, it's not rocket science what I'm doing here, but it's being aware and it has to be top of mind that you can contrast your claims, contrast your gifts, and then it becomes a natural ability and a natural way in your communication. And you basically amplify everything you're doing. Jason. Hi, boss. Can you just go back to the social proof one? I just, this is really interesting. I think when you said, if you can't find contrast, you're not looking hard enough, but I think that sometimes where to look is through your own experience. And I just wanted to just highlight that that sometimes the answer is what we experience as a consumer because this, the second option here is what I do on Amazon all the time. I go, it's four out of five and then they check how many reviews are there. And so if there was like 285 reviews versus 5000 reviews, then I'm persuaded to go or I'll tell my wife, well, there's 5000 reviews at 4.7. And so I just wanted to just highlight that because it just occurred to me that sometimes we're stuck trying to think of something out of the box or be creative. And sometimes we can just leverage off our own experience when we're influenced. And I just wanted to highlight that. So thank you. Thanks for that, Adon. And great indeed. And my last example is with the principle of scarcity. A great scarcity frame is these are the last three items available for this price. Perfect way to activate scarcity. How can we amplify this? I already see some people smiling. They got some IDs probably. There are not just 10 items, but rather three remaining available at this price. And now I like this because actually what I should have written as an example, we had a hundred items for this special offer and now we only have three remaining items. Because then I not only used contrast and scarcity like I did now, then I used actually one more principle. Social proof. There were hundreds. Now there are only three. So 97 people already took this offer. So what I like here to point out, not only contrast can amplify a principle, these principles can amplify each other as well. There's just one sentence, but I check three boxes. Contrast, social proof and scarcity. There were a hundred items. Now we only have three remaining for this special price. One sentence, but in one sentence I have contrast, social proof and scarcity. And that's what we are doing here in the mastermind to come to that level. So you can create it for your own influence challenge, your colleagues and your clients. So to wrap up my presentation and I still some time left for a few questions. Three things, sleuths of influence, this detective we are do. We know and we act like it. We know contrast is always available. We know contrast is an amplifier for each principle. So if we generate an idea how to use a principle, we can, we always think one step further. Can we amplify it with contrast? The answer is always yes, because C.1 is always available. So it becomes how do we amplify it with contrast? And three, contrast should be a default way of communication. Try to train yourself just as the seven examples I did. This is just pointing out and amplifying what you are doing. And you can do this all the time. And once it becomes a default way of communicating, first of all, you will see the effect. But second of all, it's in your rhythm. You don't have to think how do I activate the principle and now how do I amplify it with contrast? You start just doing it. You become a natural talent. It was interesting because I was really applying it to my recent encounter. Like an initiative that finally I had success with regarding moving on in the direction working with the school districts. And I use the principle of contrast when I send my proposal and I only put two offers. I started with the higher one, which had all the bells and whistles. And then the second option was free only because the previous conversation kind of went in the direction that they couldn't find the money yet. It's too complicated. So I put the free offer second and sure enough, they did go for free offer and they said, sorry, we, you know, we can't afford it right now. So we have too many budget constraints, but you know, let's do it as a standalone. So on one, you know, hand, it was kind of like, okay, it didn't work out. So make sure you at least prepare two proposals and concessions is about the moment of power. If you get a no, you don't want to go back.
